feedback.text: The upcoming EU Digital Fairness Act presents a vital opportunity to correct a growing imbalance in the digital economy particularly in how digital entertainment is treated. While the Act rightly aims to strengthen consumer rights and curb unfair digital practices, it must also address a critical, overlooked issue: the permanent loss of digital games. Across Europe, gamers are increasingly seeing games they paid for become unplayable, delisted, or remotely deactivated without warning or accountability. Titles are removed from storefronts due to expired licenses, DRM servers are shut down, and online-only requirements render entire libraries inaccessible. Some games are even deliberately "killed" by publishers not for legal reasons, but to force migration to newer products or subscription models. This isnt just a niche complaint gaming is the largest entertainment industry in Europe, generating billions annually. A generation raised on digital downloads and live services now faces the very real threat of digital cultural erasure. When a game is pulled from existence, consumers lose access to what they paid for, and society loses a piece of interactive culture. We believe the Digital Fairness Act must: Guarantee access to digital games that were lawfully purchased, even if support or sales end. Ban practices that intentionally render games unplayable after purchase unless required by law or court order. Protect game preservation through legally recognized exemptions, allowing trusted organizations to maintain and restore games no longer supported by publishers. Enforce transparency when games are removed or disabled, including clear communication and optional refunds or offline alternatives where feasible. Without these safeguards, digital games are not really "owned" they're leased under shifting terms controlled by publishers. Thats not fair, and it violates the very principle of consumer digital sovereignty that the EU claims to uphold. Europe has long stood for user rights, fair competition, and cultural heritage. Its time the law reflects that in the digital world. We urge EU lawmakers to ensure the Digital Fairness Act protects not just purchases, but the right to preserve and access our digital history including games.
